point_001: The practical advantage of the proposed method is unclear, since it achieves only similar (not superior) performance to span-aware pre-training models like SpanBERT, while adding complexity to the fine-tuning stage rather than the one-time pre-training stage.
point_002: The improvement over baselines is marginal (~1% on average), and the paper does not report mean and standard deviation across multiple runs, making it difficult to assess statistical significance given the known high variance of BERT fine-tuning on GLUE.
point_003: The experiments are limited to BERT-base and BERT-large, which are considered relatively weak baselines; results on stronger models such as RoBERTa are absent.
point_004: The method is evaluated only on sentence-level classification tasks (GLUE benchmark); it is not clear how it would extend to token-level tasks such as named-entity recognition that require word-level representations.
point_005: There is no comparison with spans extracted by an off-the-shelf syntactic chunker, leaving open the question of whether the n-gram dictionary approach is better or worse than linguistically motivated span extraction.
point_006: The effect of varying the n-gram dictionary (e.g., different n-gram orders or vocabulary sizes) on downstream performance is not analyzed.
point_007: The paper does not include experiments on entity-centric tasks (e.g., following the setup of LUKE), which would be a natural and informative evaluation for a span-representation method.
point_008: The novelty is limited because combining span-level representations has been extensively studied in prior work (e.g., Toshniwal et al., ACL RepL4NLP 2020), and the relationship to and differences from such work are not adequately discussed.
point_009: There is no fine-grained qualitative or quantitative analysis comparing which specific linguistic phenomena or task aspects the proposed method improves relative to SpanBERT, making it hard to understand complementary strengths.
point_010: It is not investigated whether applying the proposed span adaptation on top of a span-aware pre-trained model (e.g., SpanBERT) yields additional gains over standard fine-tuning of that model.
point_011: A minor typographical error exists in Formula (1): the subscript 'j' in the second line should be '1'.
